q: Glucose is a type of simple carbohydrate and the main source of energy used by body cells.
e: Glucose is a type of simple carbohydrate and the main source of energy used by the body cells.

q: Dietary glucose is primarily derived from the breakdown of proteins in the diet.
e: Dietary glucose is derived from the carbohydrates in the breakdown & the diet.

q: The two hormones that directly regulate blood glucose are glucagon and insulin.
e: The two hormones that directly regulate blood glucose are glucagon and insulin.

q: Diabetes is a disease in which blood sugar levels are consistently low.
e: Diabetes is a disease in which blood sugar levels are consistently high.

q: Diabetes mellitus (DM) causes damage to organs such as the kidneys, heart, and retina.
e: DM causes damage to organs such as the kidneys, heart, small arteries, eyes (retina).

q: Hypoglycemia refers to a sudden drop in blood sugar.
e: People with DM experience a sudden drop in blood sugar called hypoglycemia.

q: Glucose can only be measured in whole blood.
e: Glucose can be measured in whole blood or serum (or plasma).

q: Blood and hemolyzed blood are highly recommended for use as a sample for glucose testing.
e: Blood and hemolyzed blood are not recommended for use as a sample.

q: Freshly separated serum is the preferred sample for glucose measurement.
e: Freshly serum is the preferred sample.

q: The stability of glucose in serum or plasma is 7 days at 20-25°C.
e: Stability: serum, heparin or EDTA plasma... 2 days at 20-25°C, 7 days at 2-8°C.

q: At room temperature (25°C), a loss of 1 to 2% of total glucose per hour occurs in whole blood samples.
e: At room temperature (25°C), a loss of 1 to 2% of total glucose per hour is seen in whole blood samples.

q: If blood is allowed to clot without separating the cells, the glucose remains stable.
e: If the blood is allowed to clot, the glucose in the sample gets metabolized by the blood cells unless the cells are separated.

q: Fasting Blood Glucose (FBG) is measured after overnight fasting.
e: Fasting Blood Glucose (FBG): this is measured after overnight fasting.

q: Blood glucose levels are normally at their highest early in the morning after fasting.
e: Blood glucose normally is lowest early in the morning after 6 to 8 hours of fasting overnight.

q: Postprandial Blood Glucose (PPG) is taken hours after a carbohydrate-rich full meal.
e: Postprandial Blood Glucose (PPG): after a carbohydrate-rich full meal, hours are allowed to elapse before blood is taken again.

q: The Oral Glucose Tolerance Test (OGTT) evaluates the body's glucose handling after fixed oral glucose intake.
e: Oral Glucose Tolerance Test (OGTT): a fixed amount of up orally, and repeated blood sugar tests performed to check on the body's glucose handling.

q: Glycosylated Hemoglobin (HbA1C) provides an idea about blood glucose fluctuations over the last month.
e: Glycosylated Hemoglobin (HbA1C): this test gives an idea about fluctuations of glucose in blood over a period of the last three months.

q: The primary purpose of determining blood glucose is for the diagnosis of diabetes.
e: The main purpose of the determination of blood glucose is the diagiosis of diabetes.

q: Increased fasting blood glucose levels are an indication of diabetes.
e: Increased fasting blood glucose levels are an indication of diabetes.

q: Type I Diabetes is characterized by insulin resistance.
e: Type I Diabetes is characterized by Beta Cell Destruction and Absolute Insulin Deficiency. Type II involves Insulin Resistance.

q: Type II Diabetes involves a relative insulin deficiency.
e: Type II Diabetes involves Insulin Resistance and Relative Insulin Deficiency.

q: A fasting glucose level less than 70 mg/dL is classified as normal.
e: A Glucose Level Less than 70 mg/dL is classified as Hypoglycemia.

q: A fasting glucose level of 70-120 mg/dL is considered the normal range.
e: Glucose Level 70-120 mg/dL is the Normal Range.

q: A fasting glucose level of 110-120 mg/dL indicates a high risk of developing Type 2 Diabetes.
e: Glucose Level 110-120 mg/dL is classified as Prediabetes, meaning High risk of developing Type 2 Diabetes.

q: A fasting glucose level greater than 120 mg/dL indicates the presence of diabetes.
e: Glucose Level > 120 mg/dL indicates presence of diabetes.

q: The GOD-POD method stands for Glucose Oxidase-Peroxidase method.
e: The GOD-POD method, standing for Glucose Oxidase-Peroxidase method.

q: In the GOD-POD method, glucose is determined after enzymatic reduction by glucose oxidase.
e: Glucose is determined after enzymatic oxidation by glucose oxidase (GOD).

q: Glucose Oxidase converts D-Glucose into Gluconic Acid and Hydrogen Peroxide.
e: Reaction 1: Enzymatic Oxidation shows D-Glucose converted to Gluconic Acid and Hydrogen Peroxide (H2O2) by Glucose Oxidase (GOD).

q: Under the catalysis of glucose peroxidase, hydrogen peroxide reacts to form a red-violet quinoneimine dye.
e: The hydrogen peroxide formed reacts, under the catalysis of glucose peroxidase (POD), with phenol and 4-aminophenazone to form a red-violet quinoneimine dye.

q: In the GOD-POD reaction, phenol and 4-aminophenazone are used to develop the color.
e: The hydrogen peroxide formed reacts... with phenol and 4-aminophenazone to form a red-violet quinoneimine dye.

q: A spectrophotometer is required to measure the absorbance in the GOD-POD method.
e: Materials include a Full automated auto analyzer or Spectrophotometer.

q: Reagent 1 (Buffer R1) consists of a Tris buffer at pH 7.
e: Reagent 1 (Buffer R1) Components: Tris buffer pH 7 (100 mmol/L).

q: Powder R2 contains only the substrate 4-amino-antipyrine and no enzymes.
e: Reagent 2 (Powder R2) contains Enzymes: Glucose oxidase, Peroxidase and Substrate: 4-amino-antipyrine.

q: The standard glucose concentration used in Reagent 3 (R3) is 200 mg/dl.
e: Reagent 3 (Standard R3) Standard Glucose: 100 mg/dl.

q: The optimal wave length for measuring absorbance in the GOD-POD method is 505 nm.
e: Wave length: 505 nm (490 - 550).

q: During the procedure, the sample should be incubated for 10 minutes at 37°C.
e: Mix incubate 10 minutes at 37°C or 30 minutes at room temperature.

q: The color developed in the GOD-POD method is stable for exactly 60 minutes.
e: The color is stable 30 minutes.

q: The linearity of the GOD-POD assay extends up to 500 mg/dl.
e: Linearity: up to 500 mg/dl.

q: Glucose concentration is calculated by multiplying the O.D. of the sample by the O.D. of the standard.
e: Glucose concentration = (O.D. sample / O.D. standard) * concentration of standard.

q: To convert glucose concentration from mg/dL to mmol/L, you multiply by 0.555.
e: Conversion factor: mg/dL * 0.555 = mmol/L.

q: To convert glucose concentration from mmol/L to mg/dL, you multiply by 18.
e: Conversion factor : mmol/L * 18 = mg/dL.

q: Blood glucose normally is lowest early in the evening.
e: Blood glucose normally is lowest early in the morning after 6 to 8 hours of fasting overnight.

q: Patients can self-monitor their blood glucose using hand-held portable monitors.
e: Self-Monitoring: Blood glucose can also be self-monitored by the patient using meters or hand-held portable monitors.

q: Urine glucose tests cannot be coordinated with blood glucose monitoring.
e: Urine Glucose Tests: Blood glucose is coordinated with urine glucose tests.

q: Hypoglycemia requires immediate treatment due to the danger it poses.
e: Hypoglycemia... Requires immediate treatment. Danger.

q: Imbalances of blood sugar are uncommon among patients with diabetes mellitus.
e: Imbalances of blood sugar is common among patients with diabetes mellitus (DM).

q: Dietary glucose can be derived from fruits.
e: Glucose is found in fruits and is also derived from the carbohydrates in the breakdown & the diet.

q: Glucose can also be derived from glycogen breakdown by the liver.
e: Glucose is... derived from... glycogen breakdown by the liver.

q: In the GOD-POD method procedure, 10 μl of standard or sample is mixed with 1 ml of Working Reagent.
e: The procedure table indicates 10 μl of Standard/Sample is mixed with 1 ml of Working Reagent.

q: The chemical formula for glucose is $C_{6}H_{12}O_{6}$.
e: The formula for glucose is given as $C_{6}H_{12}O_{6}$.
